model_patch:
diff --git a/suite/runsuite.cmake b/suite/runsuite.cmake
index 0ea031b9d..32c6a0cea 100644
--- a/suite/runsuite.cmake
+++ b/suite/runsuite.cmake
@@ -181,7 +181,6 @@ endif ()
 ##################################################
 # Pre-commit source file checks.
 # We do have some pre-commit hooks but we don't rely on them.
-# We also have vera++ style checking rules for C/C++ code, but we
 # keep a few checks here to cover cmake and other code.
 # We could do a GLOB_RECURSE and read every file, but that's slow, so
 # we try to construct the diff.
@@ -320,7 +319,6 @@ endif ()
 
 # CMake seems to remove carriage returns for us so we can't easily
 # check for them unless we switch to perl or python or something
-# to get the diff and check it.  The vera++ rules do check C/C++ code.
 
 # Check for trailing space.  This is a diff with an initial column for +-,
 # so we need to exclude the following cases:
diff --git a/third_party/vera++/use_vera++.cmake b/third_party/vera++/use_vera++.cmake
index c90ab0d75..c2f15d1e5 100644
--- a/third_party/vera++/use_vera++.cmake
+++ b/third_party/vera++/use_vera++.cmake
@@ -1,181 +1,2 @@
-# Boost Software License - Version 1.0 - August 17th, 2003
-#
-# Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person or organization
-# obtaining a copy of the software and accompanying documentation covered by
-# this license (the "Software") to use, reproduce, display, distribute,
-# execute, and transmit the Software, and to prepare derivative works of the
-# Software, and to permit third-parties to whom the Software is furnished to
-# do so, all subject to the following:
-#
-# The copyright notices in the Software and this entire statement, including
-# the above license grant, this restriction and the following disclaimer,
-# must be included in all copies of the Software, in whole or in part, and
-# all derivative works of the Software, unless such copies or derivative
-# works are solely in the form of machine-executable object code generated by
-# a source language processor.
-#
-# THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
-# IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
-# FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, TITLE AND NON-INFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT
-# SHALL THE COPYRIGHT HOLDERS OR ANYONE DISTRIBUTING THE SOFTWARE BE LIABLE
-# FOR ANY DAMAGES OR OTHER LIABILITY, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE,
-# ARISING FROM, OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER
-# DEALINGS IN THE SOFTWARE.
-
-# We customize this function from the vera++ package's user_vera++.cmake for
-# the following purposes:
-# 1) Pass --error instead of --warning.
-#    Xref https://bitbucket.org/verateam/vera/issues/91
-# 2) Exclude some files from the glob, in particular backup files.
-
 function(add_vera_targets_for_dynamorio)
-  # default values
-  set(target "style")
-  set(target_all "style_reports")
-  set(profile "default")
-  set(root "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}")
-  set(exclusions)
-  set(recurse OFF)
-  set(globs)
-  # parse the options
-  math(EXPR lastIdx "${ARGC} - 1")
-  set(i 0)
-  while(i LESS ${ARGC})
-    set(arg "${ARGV${i}}")
-    if("${arg}" STREQUAL "NAME")
-      vera_incr(i)
-      set(target "${ARGV${i}}")
-    elseif("${arg}" STREQUAL "NAME_ALL")
-      vera_incr(i)
-      set(target_all "${ARGV${i}}")
-    elseif("${arg}" STREQUAL "ROOT")
-      vera_incr(i)
-      set(root "${ARGV${i}}")
-    elseif("${arg}" STREQUAL "PROFILE")
-      vera_incr(i)
-      set(profile "${ARGV${i}}")
-    elseif("${arg}" STREQUAL "EXCLUSION")
-      vera_incr(i)
-      list(APPEND exclusions --exclusions "${ARGV${i}}")
-    elseif("${arg}" STREQUAL "RECURSE")
-      set(recurse ON)
-    else()
-      list(APPEND globs ${arg})
-    endif()
-    vera_incr(i)
-  endwhile()
-
-  if(recurse)
-    file(GLOB_RECURSE srcs ${globs})
-  else()
-    file(GLOB srcs ${globs})
-  endif()
-  list(SORT srcs)
-
-  if(NOT VERA++_EXECUTABLE AND TARGET vera)
-    set(vera_program "$<TARGET_FILE:vera>")
-  else()
-    set(vera_program "${VERA++_EXECUTABLE}")
-  endif()
-
-  # Two custom targets will be created:
-  # * style_reports is run as part of the build, and is not rerun unless one of
-  # the file checked is modified;
-  # * style must be explicitly called (make style) and is rerun even if the files
-  # to check have not been modified. To achieve this behavior, the commands used
-  # in this target pretend to produce a file without actually producing it.
-  # Because the output file is not there after the run, the command will be rerun
-  # again at the next target build.
-  # The report style is selected based on the build environment, so the style
-  # problems are properly reported in the IDEs
-  if(MSVC)
-    set(style vc)
-  else()
-    set(style std)
-  endif()
-  set(xmlreports)
-  set(noreports)
-  set(reportNb 0)
-  set(reportsrcs)
-  list(GET srcs 0 first)
-  get_filename_component(currentDir ${first} PATH)
-  # add a fake src file in a fake dir to trigger the creation of the last
-  # custom command
-  list(APPEND srcs "#12345678900987654321#/0987654321#1234567890")
-  foreach(s ${srcs})
-    if (NOT s MATCHES "\\.#" AND # avoid emacs backup files
-        # We also exclude files in make/style_checks/exclusions here to speed
-        # up the build slightly and to handle vera++ 1.2.1 better (1.2.1 does not
-        # support regex exclusions).
-        NOT s MATCHES "/suite/tests" AND
-        NOT s MATCHES "/libutil/" AND
-        NOT s MATCHES "/tools/" AND
-        NOT s MATCHES "/third_party/" AND
-        # Somehow on Travis vera checks build-dir files.
-        NOT s MATCHES "/build_" AND
-        NOT s MATCHES ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR} AND
-        NOT s MATCHES "/install/" AND
-        # We check out drmemory for package builds.
-        NOT s MATCHES "/drmemory/")
-      get_filename_component(d ${s} PATH)
-      if(NOT "${d}" STREQUAL "${currentDir}")
-        # this is a new dir - lets generate everything needed for the previous dir
-        string(LENGTH "${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}" len)
-        string(SUBSTRING "${currentDir}" 0 ${len} pre)
-        if("${pre}" STREQUAL "${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}")
-          string(SUBSTRING "${currentDir}" ${len} -1 currentDir)
-          string(REGEX REPLACE "^/" "" currentDir "${currentDir}")
-        endif()
-        if("${currentDir}" STREQUAL "")
-          set(currentDir ".")
-        endif()
-        set(xmlreport ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/vera_report_${reportNb}.xml)
-        if (VERA_ERROR)
-          set(error_or_warning --error)
-        else ()
-          set(error_or_warning --warning)
-        endif (VERA_ERROR)
-        add_custom_command(
-          OUTPUT ${xmlreport}
-          COMMAND ${vera_program}
-            --root ${root}
-            --profile ${profile}
-            --${style}-report=-
-            --show-rule
-            ${error_or_warning}
-            --xml-report=${xmlreport}
-            ${exclusions}
-            ${reportsrcs}
-          DEPENDS ${reportsrcs}
-          COMMENT "Checking style with vera++ in ${currentDir}"
-        )
-        set(noreport ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/vera_noreport_${reportNb}.xml)
-        add_custom_command(
-          OUTPUT ${noreport}
-          COMMAND ${vera_program}
-            --root ${root}
-            --profile ${profile}
-            --${style}-report=-
-            --show-rule
-            ${error_or_warning}
-            # --xml-report=${noreport}
-            ${exclusions}
-            ${reportsrcs}
-          DEPENDS ${reportsrcs}
-          COMMENT "Checking style with vera++ in ${currentDir}"
-        )
-        list(APPEND xmlreports ${xmlreport})
-        list(APPEND noreports ${noreport})
-        vera_incr(reportNb)
-        # clear the list for the next dir
-        set(reportsrcs)
-        set(currentDir ${d})
-      endif()
-      list(APPEND reportsrcs ${s})
-    endif ()
-  endforeach()
-  # Create the custom targets that will trigger the custom command created
-  # previously
-  add_custom_target(${target_all} ALL DEPENDS ${xmlreports})
-  add_custom_target(${target} DEPENDS ${noreports})
 endfunction()
reproduction_file: cat: /testbed/reproduction.py: No such file or directory

content.text: I'll help you fix this bug related to non-alarm signals accumulating and causing lock problems. Let me start by exploring the codebase to understand the issue.

content.content:
Entered plan mode. You should now focus on exploring the codebase and designing an implementation approach.

In plan mode, you should:
1. Thoroughly explore the codebase to understand existing patterns
2. Identify similar features and architectural approaches
3. Consider multiple approaches and their trade-offs
4. Use AskUserQuestion if you need to clarify the approach
5. Design a concrete implementation strategy
6. When ready, use ExitPlanMode to present your plan for approval

Remember: DO NOT write or edit any files yet. This is a read-only exploration and planning phase.

tool_use_result.message: Entered plan mode. You should now focus on exploring the codebase and designing an implementation approach.

content.text: (no content)

input.prompt:
Explore the codebase to understand how signal handling works, particularly focusing on:
1. Find files related to signal handling (Unix signals, pending signals)
2. Look for the code that handles pending signals and the limit mentioned in the bug report (max_pending_signals = 8)
3. Find where alarm signals are handled differently (dropped at 3rd pending)
4. Look for the record_pending_signal function mentioned in the stack trace
5. Search for files mentioned in the stack trace: signal.c, vmareas.c, heap.c, memquery_linux.c
6. Understand the lock ordering issue described in the bug report

Please be thorough and provide detailed findings about the signal handling mechanism and the specific issue described.

content.text:
Explore the codebase to understand how signal handling works, particularly focusing on:

3. Find where alarm signals are handled differently (dropped at 3rd pending)
4. Look for the record_pending_signal function mentioned in the stack trace
5. Search for files mentioned in the stack trace: signal.c, vmareas.c, heap.c, memquery_linux.c
6. Understand the lock ordering issue described in the bug report

Please be thorough and provide detailed findings about the signal handling mechanism and the specific issue described.

   106→ * in to the non-rt sigaction() call, which we do not yet support)

   117→    kernel_sigset_t mask;
   118→    int flags;

   151→/* kernel's notion of ucontext is different from glibc's!

   204→#if defined(LINUX) || defined(X64)

   214→#ifdef LINUX
   215→/* we assume frames look like this, with rt_sigframe used if SA_SIGINFO is set
   216→ * (these are from /usr/src/linux/arch/i386/kernel/signal.c for kernel 2.4.17)
   217→ */
   218→
   219→#    if defined(X86)
   220→#        define RETCODE_SIZE 8
   221→#    elif defined(ARM)
   222→#        define RETCODE_SIZE 16
   223→#    endif
   224→
   225→typedef struct sigframe {
   226→#    ifdef X86
   227→    char *pretcode;
   228→    int sig;
   229→    sigcontext_t sc;
   230→    /* Since 2.6.28, this fpstate has been unused and the real fpstate
   231→     * is at the end of the struct so it can include xstate
   232→     */
   233→    kernel_fpstate_t fpstate;
   234→    unsigned long extramask[_NSIG_WORDS - 1];
   235→    char retcode[RETCODE_SIZE];
   236→#    elif defined(ARM)
   237→    kernel_ucontext_t uc;
   238→    char retcode[RETCODE_SIZE];
   239→#    endif
   240→    /* XXX: this is a field I added, so our frame looks different from
   241→     * the kernel's...but where else can I store sig where the app won't
   242→     * clobber it?
   243→     * WARNING: our handler receives only rt frames, and we construct
   244→     * plain frames but never pass them to the kernel (on sigreturn() we
   245→     * just go to new context and interpret from there), so the only
   246→     * transparency problem here is if app tries to build its own plain
   247→     * frame and call sigreturn() unrelated to signal delivery.
   248→     * UPDATE: actually we do invoke SYS_*sigreturn
   249→     */
   250→    int sig_noclobber;
   251→    /* In 2.6.28+, fpstate/xstate goes here */
   252→} sigframe_plain_t;
   253→#else
   254→/* Mac only has one frame type, with a libc stub that calls 1-arg or 3-arg handler */
   255→#endif
   256→
   257→/* the rt frame is used for SA_SIGINFO signals */
   258→typedef struct rt_sigframe {
   259→#ifdef LINUX
   260→#    ifdef X86
   261→    char *pretcode;
   262→#        ifdef X64
   263→#            ifdef VMX86_SERVER
   264→    kernel_siginfo_t info;
   265→    kernel_ucontext_t uc;
   266→#            else
   267→    kernel_ucontext_t uc;
   268→    kernel_siginfo_t info;
   269→#            endif
   270→#        else
   271→    int sig;
   272→    kernel_siginfo_t *pinfo;
   273→    void *puc;
   274→    kernel_siginfo_t info;
   275→    kernel_ucontext_t uc;
   276→    /* Prior to 2.6.28, "kernel_fpstate_t fpstate" was here.  Rather than
   277→     * try to reproduce that exact layout and detect the underlying kernel
   278→     * (the safest way would be to send ourselves a signal and examine the
   279→     * frame, rather than relying on uname, to handle backports), we use
   280→     * the new layout even on old kernels.  The app should use the fpstate
   281→     * pointer in the sigcontext anyway.
   282→     */
   283→    char retcode[RETCODE_SIZE];
   284→#        endif
   285→    /* In 2.6.28+, fpstate/xstate goes here */
   286→#    elif defined(AARCHXX)
   287→    kernel_siginfo_t info;
   288→    kernel_ucontext_t uc;
   289→#    elif defined(RISCV64)
   290→    kernel_siginfo_t info;
   291→    kernel_ucontext_t uc;
   292→#    endif
   293→
   294→#elif defined(MACOS)
   295→#    ifdef AARCH64
   296→    kernel_siginfo_t info;
   297→    struct __darwin_ucontext64 uc;
   298→    struct __darwin_mcontext64 mc;
   299→#    elif defined(X64)
   300→    /* Kernel places padding to align to 16 (via an inefficient alignment macro!),
   301→     * and then skips the retaddr slot to align to 8.
   302→     */
   303→    /* TODO i#1979/i#1312: This will be __darwin_mcontext_avx512_64 if AVX512 is
   304→     * enabled.  Given that it's inlined here *first*, though, we need to figure
   305→     * out how best to handle this variability.  Multiple sigframe_rt_t struct
   306→     * definitions?  Do we want a discovery signal to find the size at init time
   307→     * like on Linux?  We would get the size by counting from "info".
   308→     * Also, should we change this to sigcontext_t.
   309→     */
   310→    struct __darwin_mcontext_avx64 mc; /* sigcontext, "struct mcontext_avx64" to kernel */
   311→    kernel_siginfo_t info;             /* matches user-mode sys/signal.h struct */
   312→    struct __darwin_ucontext64 uc;     /* "struct user_ucontext64" to kernel */
   313→#    else
   314→    app_pc retaddr;
   315→    app_pc handler;
   316→    int sigstyle; /* UC_TRAD = 1-arg, UC_FLAVOR = 3-arg handler */
   317→    int sig;
   318→    kernel_siginfo_t *pinfo;
   319→    struct __darwin_ucontext *puc; /* "struct user_ucontext32 *" to kernel */
   320→    /* The kernel places padding here to align to 16 and then subtract one slot
   321→     * for retaddr post-call alignment, so don't access these subsequent fields
   322→     * directly if given a frame from the kernel!
   323→     */
   324→    struct __darwin_mcontext_avx32 mc; /* sigcontext, "struct mcontext_avx32" to kernel */
   325→    kernel_siginfo_t info;             /* matches user-mode sys/signal.h struct */
   326→    struct __darwin_ucontext uc;       /* "struct user_ucontext32" to kernel */
   327→#    endif
   328→#endif
   329→} sigframe_rt_t;
   330→
   331→/* we have to queue up both rt and non-rt signals because we delay
   332→ * their delivery.
   333→ * PR 304708: we now leave in rt form right up until we copy to the
   334→ * app stack, so that we can deliver to a client at a safe spot
   335→ * in rt form.
   336→ */
   337→typedef struct _sigpending_t {
   338→    sigframe_rt_t rt_frame;
   339→    /* i#182/PR 449996: we provide the faulting access address for SIGSEGV, etc. */
   340→    byte *access_address;
   341→    /* use the sigcontext, not the mcontext (used to restart syscalls for i#1145) */
   342→    bool use_sigcontext;
   343→    /* Was this unblocked at receive time? */
   344→    bool unblocked_at_receipt;
   345→    struct _sigpending_t *next;
   346→#if defined(LINUX) && defined(X86)
   347→    /* fpstate is no longer kept inside the frame, and is not always present.
   348→     * if we delay we need to ensure we have room for it.
   349→     * we statically keep room for full xstate in case we need it.
   350→     */
   351→    kernel_xstate_t __attribute__((aligned(AVX_ALIGNMENT))) xstate;
   352→    /* The xstate struct grows and we have to allow for variable sizing,
   353→     * which we handle here by placing it last.
   354→     */
   355→#endif /* LINUX && X86 */
   356→} sigpending_t;

   362→ * PER-THREAD DATA
   363→ */
   364→
   365→/* PR 204556: DR/clients use itimers so we need to emulate the app's usage */
   366→typedef struct _itimer_info_t {
   367→    /* easier to manipulate a single value than the two-field struct timeval */
   368→    uint64 interval;
   369→    uint64 value;
   370→} itimer_info_t;
   371→
   372→typedef struct _thread_itimer_info_t {
   373→    /* We use per-itimer-signal-type locks to avoid races with alarms arriving
   374→     * in separate threads simultaneously (we don't want to block on itimer
   375→     * locks to handle app-syscall-interruption cases).  Xref i#2993.
   376→     * We only need owner info -- xref i#219: we should add a known-owner
   377→     * lock for cases where a full-fledged recursive lock is not needed.
   378→     */
   379→    recursive_lock_t lock;
   380→    itimer_info_t app;
   381→    itimer_info_t app_saved;
   382→    itimer_info_t dr;
   383→    itimer_info_t actual;
   384→    void (*cb)(dcontext_t *, priv_mcontext_t *);
   385→    /* version for clients */
   386→    void (*cb_api)(dcontext_t *, dr_mcontext_t *);
   387→} thread_itimer_info_t;
   388→
   389→/* We use all 3: ITIMER_REAL for clients (i#283/PR 368737), ITIMER_VIRTUAL
   390→ * for -prof_pcs, and ITIMER_PROF for PAPI
   391→ */
   392→#define NUM_ITIMERS 3
   393→
   394→/* Don't try to translate every alarm if they're piling up (PR 213040) */
   395→#define SKIP_ALARM_XL8_MAX 3
   396→
   397→struct _sigfd_pipe_t;
   398→typedef struct _sigfd_pipe_t sigfd_pipe_t;
   399→
   400→/* Data that is shared for all threads in a CLONE_SIGHAND group.
   401→ * Typically this is the whole thread group or "process".
   402→ * The thread_sig_info_t for each thread in the group points to a
   403→ * single copy of this structure.
   404→ */
   405→typedef struct _sighand_info_t {
   406→    bool is_shared;
   407→    int refcount;
   408→    mutex_t lock;
   409→    /* We use kernel_sigaction_t so we don't have to translate back and forth
   410→     * between it and the libc version.
   411→     */
   412→    kernel_sigaction_t *action[SIGARRAY_SIZE];
   413→    bool we_intercept[SIGARRAY_SIZE];
   414→    /* For handling masked-for-app-but-not-for-DR signals.  Any time we receive
   415→     * a signal in a thread for which it is blocked, we need to know whether it was
   416→     * a "process"-wide signal and whether some other thread has it unblocked.
   417→     * To avoid heavyweight locks every time, we keep an atomic-access counter of
   418→     * unmasked threads for each signal, for the CLONE_SIGHAND group (typically
   419→     * whole process).
   420→     */
   421→    int threads_unmasked[SIGARRAY_SIZE];
   422→} sighand_info_t;
   423→
   424→typedef struct _thread_sig_info_t {
   425→    /* A pointer to handler info shared in a CLONG_SIGHAND group. */
   426→    sighand_info_t *sighand;
   427→
   428→    /* We save the old sigaction across a sigaction syscall so we can return it
   429→     * in post-syscall handling.
   430→     */
   431→    kernel_sigaction_t prior_app_sigaction;
   432→    bool use_kernel_prior_sigaction;
   433→    /* We pass this to the kernel in lieu of the app's data struct, so we
   434→     * can modify it.
   435→     */
   436→    kernel_sigaction_t our_sigaction;
   437→    /* This is the app's sigaction pointer, for restoring post-syscall. */
   438→    const kernel_sigaction_t *sigaction_param;
   439→
   440→    /* True after signal_thread_inherit or signal_fork_init are called.  We
   441→     * squash alarm or profiling signals up until this point.
   442→     */
   443→    bool fully_initialized;
   444→
   445→    /* DR and clients use itimers, so we need to emulate the app's itimer
   446→     * usage.  This info is shared across CLONE_THREAD threads only for
   447→     * NPTL in kernel 2.6.12+ so these fields are separately shareable from
   448→     * the CLONE_SIGHAND set of fields above.
   449→     */
   450→    bool shared_itimer;
   451→    /* Because a non-CLONE_THREAD thread can be created we can't just use
   452→     * dynamo_exited and need a refcount here.  This is updated via
   453→     * atomic inc/dec without holding a lock (i#1993).
   454→     */
   455→    int *shared_itimer_refcount;
   456→    /* Indicates the # of threads under DR control.  This is updated via atomic
   457→     * inc/dec without holding a lock (i#1993).
   458→     */
   459→    int *shared_itimer_underDR;
   460→    thread_itimer_info_t (*itimer)[NUM_ITIMERS];
   461→
   462→    /* cache restorer validity.  not shared: inheriter will re-populate. */
   463→    int restorer_valid[SIGARRAY_SIZE];
   464→
   465→    /* rest of app state */
   466→    stack_t app_sigstack;
   467→    sigpending_t *sigpending[SIGARRAY_SIZE];
   468→    /* count of pending signals */
   469→    int num_pending;
   470→    /* are the pending still on one special heap unit? */
   471→    bool multiple_pending_units;
   472→    /* "lock" to prevent interrupting signal from messing up sigpending array */
   473→    bool accessing_sigpending;
   474→    bool nested_pending_ok;
   475→
   476→    /* This thread's application signal mask: the set of blocked signals.
   477→     * We need to keep this in sync with the thread-group-shared
   478→     * sighand->threads_unmasked.
   479→     *
   480→     * reroute_to_unmasked_thread() needs read access to app_sigblocked from other
   481→     * threads.  However, we also need lockless read access from our signal handler.
   482→     * Since all writes are from the owning thread, we read w/o a lock from the owning
   483→     * thread, but use the lock for writes from the owning thread and reads from
   484→     * other threads.  (The bitwise operations make it difficult to use atomic
   485→     * updates instead of a mutex.)
   486→     */
   487→    kernel_sigset_t app_sigblocked;
   488→    mutex_t sigblocked_lock;
   489→    /* This is a not-guaranteed-accurate indicator of whether we're inside an
   490→     * app signal handler.  We can't know for sure when a handler ends if the
   491→     * app exits with a longjmp instead of siglongjmp.
   492→     */
   493→    bool in_app_handler;
   494→
   495→    /* for returning the old mask (xref PR 523394) */
   496→    kernel_sigset_t pre_syscall_app_sigblocked;
   497→    /* for preserving the app memory (xref i#1187), and for preserving app
   498→     * mask supporting ppoll, epoll_pwait and pselect
   499→     */
   500→    kernel_sigset_t pre_syscall_app_sigprocmask;
   501→    /* True if pre_syscall_app_sigprocmask holds a pre-syscall sigmask */
   502→    bool pre_syscall_app_sigprocmask_valid;
   503→    /* for alarm signals arriving in coarse units we only attempt to xl8
   504→     * every nth signal since coarse translation is expensive (PR 213040)
   505→     */
   506→    uint skip_alarm_xl8;
   507→    /* signalfd array (lazily initialized) */
   508→    sigfd_pipe_t *signalfd[SIGARRAY_SIZE];
   509→
   510→    /* to handle sigsuspend we have to save blocked set */
   511→    bool in_sigsuspend;
   512→    kernel_sigset_t app_sigblocked_save;
   513→
   514→    /* to inherit in children must not modify until they're scheduled */
   515→    volatile int num_unstarted_children;
   516→    mutex_t child_lock;
   517→
   518→    /* our own structures */
   519→    stack_t sigstack;
   520→    void *sigheap;           /* special heap */
   521→    fragment_t *interrupted; /* frag we unlinked for delaying signal */
   522→    cache_pc interrupted_pc; /* pc within frag we unlinked for delaying signal */
   523→
   524→#if defined(X86) && defined(LINUX)
   525→    /* As the xstate buffer varies dynamically and gets large (with avx512
   526→     * it is over 2K) we use a copy on the heap.  There are paths where we
   527→     * can't easily free it locally so we keep a pointer in the TLS.
   528→     */
   529→    byte *xstate_buf;   /* xstate_alloc aligned */
   530→    byte *xstate_alloc; /* unaligned */
   531→#endif
   532→
   533→#ifdef RETURN_AFTER_CALL
   534→    app_pc signal_restorer_retaddr; /* last signal restorer, known ret exception */
   535→#endif
   536→} thread_sig_info_t;

   538→/***************************************************************************
   539→ * GENERAL ROUTINES (in signal.c)
   540→ */
   541→
   542→sigcontext_t *
   543→get_sigcontext_from_rt_frame(sigframe_rt_t *frame);
   544→
   545→/**** kernel_sigset_t ***************************************************/
   546→
   547→/* defines and typedefs are exported in os_exports.h for siglongjmp */
   548→
   549→/* For MacOS, the type is really __darwin_sigset_t, which is a plain __uint32_t.
   550→ * We stick with the struct-containing-uint to simplify the helpers here.
   551→ */
   552→
   553→/* most of these are from /usr/src/linux/include/linux/signal.h */
   554→static inline void
   555→kernel_sigemptyset(kernel_sigset_t *set)
   556→{
   557→    memset(set, 0, sizeof(kernel_sigset_t));
   558→}
   559→
   560→static inline void
   561→kernel_sigfillset(kernel_sigset_t *set)
   562→{
   563→    memset(set, -1, sizeof(kernel_sigset_t));
   564→}
   565→
   566→static inline void
   567→kernel_sigaddset(kernel_sigset_t *set, int _sig)
   568→{
   569→    uint sig = _sig - 1;
   570→    if (_NSIG_WORDS == 1)
   571→        set->sig[0] |= 1UL << sig;
   572→    else
   573→        set->sig[sig / _NSIG_BPW] |= 1UL << (sig % _NSIG_BPW);
   574→}
   575→
   576→static inline void
   577→kernel_sigdelset(kernel_sigset_t *set, int _sig)
   578→{
   579→    uint sig = _sig - 1;
   580→    if (_NSIG_WORDS == 1)
   581→        set->sig[0] &= ~(1UL << sig);
   582→    else
   583→        set->sig[sig / _NSIG_BPW] &= ~(1UL << (sig % _NSIG_BPW));
   584→}
   585→
   586→static inline bool
   587→kernel_sigismember(kernel_sigset_t *set, int _sig)
   588→{
   589→    int sig = _sig - 1; /* go to 0-based */
   590→    if (_NSIG_WORDS == 1)
   591→        return CAST_TO_bool(1 & (set->sig[0] >> sig));
   592→    else
   593→        return CAST_TO_bool(1 & (set->sig[sig / _NSIG_BPW] >> (sig % _NSIG_BPW)));
   594→}
   595→
   596→/* XXX: how does libc do this? */
   597→static inline void
   598→copy_kernel_sigset_to_sigset(kernel_sigset_t *kset, sigset_t *uset)
   599→{
   600→    int sig;
   601→#ifdef DEBUG
   602→    int rc =
   603→#endif
   604→        sigemptyset(uset);
   605→    ASSERT(rc == 0);
   606→    /* do this the slow way...I don't want to make assumptions about
   607→     * structure of user sigset_t
   608→     */
   609→    for (sig = 1; sig <= MAX_SIGNUM; sig++) {
   610→        if (kernel_sigismember(kset, sig))
   611→            sigaddset(uset, sig); /* inlined, so no libc dep */
   612→    }
   613→}
   614→
   615→/* i#1541: unfortunately sigismember now leads to libc imports so we write our own */
   616→static inline bool
   617→libc_sigismember(const sigset_t *set, int _sig)
   618→{
   619→    int sig = _sig - 1; /* go to 0-based */
   620→#if defined(MACOS) || defined(ANDROID32)
   621→    /* sigset_t is just a uint32 */
   622→    return TEST(1UL << sig, *set);
   623→#else
   624→    /* "set->__val" would be cleaner, but is glibc specific (e.g. musl libc
   625→     * uses __bits as the field name on sigset_t).
   626→     */
   627→    uint bits_per = 8 * sizeof(ulong);
   628→    return TEST(1UL << (sig % bits_per), ((const ulong *)set)[sig / bits_per]);
   629→#endif
   630→}
   631→
   632→/* XXX: how does libc do this? */
   633→static inline void
   634→copy_sigset_to_kernel_sigset(const sigset_t *uset, kernel_sigset_t *kset)
   635→{
   636→    int sig;
   637→    kernel_sigemptyset(kset);
   638→    for (sig = 1; sig <= MAX_SIGNUM; sig++) {
   639→        if (libc_sigismember(uset, sig))
   640→            kernel_sigaddset(kset, sig);
   641→    }
   642→}
   643→
   644→int
   645→sigaction_syscall(int sig, kernel_sigaction_t *act, kernel_sigaction_t *oact);

/*
 * heap.c - heap manager
 */

#include "globals.h"
#include <limits.h>

#include "fragment.h" /* for struct sizes */
#include "link.h"     /* for struct sizes */
#include "instr.h"    /* for struct sizes */
#include "fcache.h"   /* fcache_low_on_memory */
#ifdef UNIX
#    include "memquery.h"
#endif
#ifdef DEBUG
#    include "hotpatch.h" /* To handle leak for case 9593. */
#endif
#include "instrument.h"

#ifdef HEAP_ACCOUNTING
#    ifndef DEBUG
#        error HEAP_ACCOUNTING requires DEBUG
#    endif
#endif

#ifdef DEBUG_MEMORY
/* on by default but higher than general asserts */
#    define CHKLVL_MEMFILL CHKLVL_DEFAULT
#endif

extern bool vm_areas_exited;

/***************************************************************************
 * we cannot use malloc in the middle of interpreting the client program
 * because we could be in the middle of interpreting malloc, which is not
 * always reentrant
 *
 * We have a virtual memory manager which makes sure memory is
 * reserved within the application address space so that we don't have
 * to fight with the application.  We call os_heap_reserve to allocate
 * virtual space in a single consecutive region.  We later use
 * os_heap_commit to get committed memory in large chunks and manage
 * the chunks using a simple scheme of free lists of different sizes.
 * The virtual memory manager has to store out of band information about
 * used and free blocks, since of course there is no real memory to use.
 * The chunks (heap units) store in band extra information both for
 * used and free.  However, in the allocated blocks within a unit we
 * don't need to store any information since heap_free passes in the
 * size; we store the next pointers for the free lists at the start of
 * the free blocks themselves.  We have one large reservation for most of
 * our allocations, and yet another for allocations that we do not
 * plan on ever freeing up on detach - the only unavoidable tombstones
 * are those for thread private code system calls that may be stuck on
 * callbacks.  In case we run out of reserved memory we do fall back
 * on requests from the OS, but any of these may fail if we are
 * competing with the application.
 *
 * looking at dynamo behavior as of Jan 2001, most heap_alloc requests are
 * for < 128 bytes, very few for larger, so we have a bunch of fixed-size
 * blocks of small sizes
 *
 * the UINT_MAX size is a variable-length block, we keep one byte to store
 * the size (again storing the next pointer when free at the start of

We went with the final option: the rseq_cs fields are filled in via a new post-emit control point, using information stored in labels during mangling. The pointer to the rseq_cs is inserted with a dummy value and patched in this new control point using a new utility routine patch_mov_immed_ptrsz().

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/page_rseq.html:228:

The patching has its own complexities. It's easiest to use insert_mov_immed_ptrsz() to point at therseq_cs. But with it being heap-allocated, that means we have to allocate it during mangling. That complicates uses of mangle that do not emit a real fragment (translation via recreation, e.g.) or block building aborts, leading to:

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/page_rseq.html-229-
  • Leaked memory. We could add a free_bb_ilist() which calls mangle_cleanup() and call it in all places that destroy bb->ilist, ensuring we free rseq_cs on all non-emit-fragment paths.
    • /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/page_rseq.html-230-
  • Potential non-determinism on translation if a new heap allocation is used which has different reachability (1 vs 2 instrs for insert_mov_immed_ptrsz()). On translation we could go look up the address used for the fragment; but what about a deleted fragment?
    • /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/page_rseq.html-231-

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html:37:

Ideally, DynamoRIO and its client's resources should be completely disjoint from the application's, to avoid conflicts in the usage of libraries, heap, input/output, and locks. Library conflicts are the most relevant to a client.

-- /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-694-

DR constructs dynamic basic blocks, which are distinct from a compiler's classic basic blocks. DR does not know all entry points ahead of time, and will end up duplicating the tail of a basic block if a later entry point is discovered that targets the middle of a block created earlier, or if a later entry point targets straight-line code that falls through into code already present in a block.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-695-

DR may call func again if it needs to translate from code cache addresses back to application addresses, which happens on faulting instructions as well as in certain situations involving suspended threads or forcibly relocated threads. The translating parameter distinguishes the two types of calls and is further explained below.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-696-

  • tag is a unique identifier for the basic block fragment. Use dr_fragment_app_pc() to translate it to an application address.
    • /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-699-
  • bb is a pointer to the list of instructions that comprise the basic block. Clients can examine, manipulate, or completely replace the instructions in the list.
    • /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-700-
  • translating indicates whether this callback is for basic block creation (false) or is for address translation (true). This is further explained below.

We went with the final option: the rseq_cs fields are filled in via a new post-emit control point, using information stored in labels during mangling. The pointer to the rseq_cs is inserted with a dummy value and patched in this new control point using a new utility routine patch_mov_immed_ptrsz().

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/page_rseq.html:228:

The patching has its own complexities. It's easiest to use insert_mov_immed_ptrsz() to point at therseq_cs. But with it being heap-allocated, that means we have to allocate it during mangling. We first describe each of the three transparency categories for DynamoRIO and their ramifications for clients using the code cache.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html-35-

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html-36-Resource Usage Conflicts

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html:37:

Ideally, DynamoRIO and its client's resources should be completely disjoint from the application's, to avoid conflicts in the usage of libraries, heap, input/output, and locks. Library conflicts are the most relevant to a client.

-- /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-694-

DR constructs dynamic basic blocks, which are distinct from a compiler's classic basic blocks. DR does not know all entry points ahead of time, and will end up duplicating the tail of a basic block if a later entry point is discovered that targets the middle of a block created earlier, or if a later entry point targets straight-line code that falls through into code already present in a block.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-695-

DR may call func again if it needs to translate from code cache addresses back to application addresses, which happens on faulting instructions as well as in certain situations involving suspended threads or forcibly relocated threads. The translating parameter distinguishes the two types of calls and is further explained below.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-696-

    • /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-699-
  • bb is a pointer to the list of instructions that comprise the basic block. Clients can examine, manipulate, or completely replace the instructions in the list.
    • /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-700-
  • translating indicates whether this callback is for basic block creation (false) or is for address translation (true). This is further explained below.

We first describe each of the three transparency categories for DynamoRIO and their ramifications for clients using the code cache.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-108-

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-109-Resource Usage Conflicts

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html:110:

Ideally, DynamoRIO and its client's resources should be completely disjoint from the application's, to avoid conflicts in the usage of libraries, heap, input/output, and locks. Library conflicts are the most relevant to a client.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-111-
Library Transparency
/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-112-[Omitted long context line] /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-113-
-- /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html-763-

DR constructs dynamic basic blocks, which are distinct from a compiler's classic basic blocks. DR does not know all entry points ahead of time, and will end up duplicating the tail of a basic block if a later entry point is discovered that targets the middle of a block created earlier, or if a later entry point targets straight-line code that falls through into code already present in a block.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html-764-

DR may call func again if it needs to translate from code cache addresses back to application addresses, which happens on faulting instructions as well as in certain situations involving suspended threads or forcibly relocated threads. The translating parameter distinguishes the two types of calls and is further explained below.

install/docs/html/page_rseq.html-300-

We went with the final option: the rseq_cs fields are filled in via a new post-emit control point, using information stored in labels during mangling. The pointer to the rseq_cs is inserted with a dummy value and patched in this new control point using a new utility routine patch_mov_immed_ptrsz().

install/docs/html/page_rseq.html:301:

The patching has its own complexities. It's easiest to use insert_mov_immed_ptrsz() to point at therseq_cs. But with it being heap-allocated, that means we have to allocate it during mangling. That complicates uses of mangle that do not emit a real fragment (translation via recreation, e.g.) or block building aborts, leading to:

    /testbed/install/docs/html/page_rseq.html-302-
  • Leaked memory. We could add a free_bb_ilist() which calls mangle_cleanup() and call it in all places that destroy bb->ilist, ensuring we free rseq_cs on all non-emit-fragment paths.
    • /testbed/install/docs/html/page_rseq.html-303-
  • Potential non-determinism on translation if a new heap allocation is used which has different reachability (1 vs 2 instrs for insert_mov_immed_ptrsz()). On translation we could go look up the address used for the fragment; but what about a deleted fragment?
    • /testbed/install/docs/html/page_rseq.html-304-

We first describe each of the three transparency categories for DynamoRIO and their ramifications for clients using the code cache.

/testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-108-

/testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-109-Resource Usage Conflicts

install/docs/html/transparency.html:110:

Ideally, DynamoRIO and its client's resources should be completely disjoint from the application's, to avoid conflicts in the usage of libraries, heap, input/output, and locks. Library conflicts are the most relevant to a client.

/testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-111-
Library Transparency
/testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-112-[Omitted long context line] /testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-113-
-- /testbed/install/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html-763-

DR constructs dynamic basic blocks, which are distinct from a compiler's classic basic blocks. DR does not know all entry points ahead of time, and will end up duplicating the tail of a basic block if a later entry point is discovered that targets the middle of a block created earlier, or if a later entry point targets straight-line code that falls through into code already present in a block.

/testbed/install/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html-764-

DR may call func again if it needs to translate from code cache addresses back to application addresses, which happens on faulting instructions as well as in certain situations involving suspended threads or forcibly relocated threads. The translating parameter distinguishes the two types of calls and is further explained below.

/testbed/install/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html-765-
    install/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html:766:[Omitted long matching line] /testbed/install/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html-767-
  • tag is a unique identifier for the basic block fragment. Use dr_fragment_app_pc() to translate it to an application address.
    • /testbed/install/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html-768-
  • bb is a pointer to the list of instructions that comprise the basic block. Clients can examine, manipulate, or completely replace the instructions in the list.
    • /testbed/install/docs/html/dr__events_8h.html-769-
  • translating indicates whether this callback is for basic block creation (false) or is for address translation (true). This is further explained below.
    • -- /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/page_rseq.html-225-[Omitted long context line] /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/page_rseq.html-226-
install/docs_embed/html/page_rseq.html-227-

We went with the final option: the rseq_cs fields are filled in via a new post-emit control point, using information stored in labels during mangling. The pointer to the rseq_cs is inserted with a dummy value and patched in this new control point using a new utility routine patch_mov_immed_ptrsz().

install/docs_embed/html/page_rseq.html:228:

The patching has its own complexities. It's easiest to use insert_mov_immed_ptrsz() to point at therseq_cs. But with it being heap-allocated, that means we have to allocate it during mangling. That complicates uses of mangle that do not emit a real fragment (translation via recreation, e.g.) or block building aborts, leading to:

    /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/page_rseq.html-229-
  • Leaked memory. We could add a free_bb_ilist() which calls mangle_cleanup() and call it in all places that destroy bb->ilist, ensuring we free rseq_cs on all non-emit-fragment paths.

We first describe each of the three transparency categories for DynamoRIO and their ramifications for clients using the code cache.

install/docs_embed/html/transparency.html:37:

Ideally, DynamoRIO and its client's resources should be completely disjoint from the application's, to avoid conflicts in the usage of libraries, heap, input/output, and locks. Library conflicts are the most relevant to a client.

-- /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-694-

DR constructs dynamic basic blocks, which are distinct from a compiler's classic basic blocks. DR does not know all entry points ahead of time, and will end up duplicating the tail of a basic block if a later entry point is discovered that targets the middle of a block created earlier, or if a later entry point targets straight-line code that falls through into code already present in a block.

/testbed/install/docs_embed/html/dr__events_8h.html-695-

DR may call func again if it needs to translate from code cache addresses back to application addresses, which happens on faulting instructions as well as in certain situations involving suspended threads or forcibly relocated threads. The translating parameter distinguishes the two types of calls and is further explained below.

The memory access traces contain some optimizations that combine references for one basic block together. This may result in not considering some thread interleavings that could occur natively. There are no other disruptions to thread ordering, however, and the application runs with all of its threads concurrently just like it would natively (although slower).

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html:90:

The memory access traces contain some optimizations that combine references for one basic block together. This may result in not considering some thread interleavings that could occur natively. There are no other disruptions to thread ordering, however, and the application runs with all of its threads concurrently just like it would natively (although slower).

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-91-

Once every process has exited, the simulator prints cache miss statistics for each cache to stderr. The simulator is designed to be extensible, allowing for different cache studies to be carried out: see Extending the Simulator.

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-92-

For L2 caching devices, the L1 caching devices are considered its children. Two separate miss rates are computed, one (the "Local miss rate") considering just requests that reach L2 while the other (the "Total miss rate") includes the child hits. This generalizes to deeper hierarchies: lower level caches are children and reported child hits are cumulative across all lower levels.

/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-93-

For memory requests that cross blocks, each block touched is considered separately, resulting in separate hit and miss statistics. This can be changed by implementing a custom statistics gatherer (see Extending the Simulator).

build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/page_rseq.html:73:[Omitted long matching line] /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/page_rseq.html-74-

Since Restartable Sequences are registered using a (relatively new) syscall, we could simply blocklist the syscall and claim that the kernel does not support the feature since most libraries will be written to be backwards-compatible for slightly older kernels. Barring that, DynamoRIO will have to support Restartable Sequences explicitly.

    DynamoRIO must pretend that it is not perturbing the application's address space. An application bug that writes to invalid memory and generates an exception should do the same thing under DynamoRIO, even if we have allocated memory at that location that would natively have been invalid. This requires protecting all DynamoRIO memory from inadvertent (or malicious) writes by the application. Furthermore, DynamoRIO hides itself from introspection by manipulating memory queries.

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html-89-
    Application Address Transparency
    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html-90-[Omitted long context line] build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html:91:[Omitted long matching line] /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html-92-

    Application errors under DynamoRIO must occur as they would natively. We accomplish this by maintaining Heap Transparency, Data Transparency, Stack Transparency, and Address Space Transparency, We have seen many cases of applications that access invalid memory natively, handle the exception, and carry on. Without Error Transparency such applications would not work properly under DynamoRIO.

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html-93-
    Timing Transparency
    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/embed/html/transparency.html-94-

    We would like to make it impossible for the application to determine whether it is executing inside of DynamoRIO. However, this may not be attainable for some aspects of execution, such as the exact timing of certain operations. This brings efficiency into the transparency equation.

    The memory access traces contain some optimizations that combine references for one basic block together. This may result in not considering some thread interleavings that could occur natively. There are no other disruptions to thread ordering, however, and the application runs with all of its threads concurrently just like it would natively (although slower).

    build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-164-

    Once every process has exited, the simulator prints cache miss statistics for each cache to stderr. The simulator is designed to be extensible, allowing for different cache studies to be carried out: see Extending the Simulator.

    build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-165-

    For L2 caching devices, the L1 caching devices are considered its children. Two separate miss rates are computed, one (the "Local miss rate") considering just requests that reach L2 while the other (the "Total miss rate") includes the child hits. This generalizes to deeper hierarchies: lower level caches are children and reported child hits are cumulative across all lower levels.

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-166-

    For memory requests that cross blocks, each block touched is considered separately, resulting in separate hit and miss statistics. This can be changed by implementing a custom statistics gatherer (see Extending the Simulator).

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-151-

    Even without a target tool like drcachesim's memory tracer inserting loads and stores, the core DynamoRIO instrumentation engine has several cases where it inserts memory operations between application instructions.

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-152-[Omitted long context line] build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/page_ldstex.html:153:[Omitted long matching line] /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-154-[Omitted long context line] /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-155-

    Additionally, application indirect branches under DynamoRIO load entries from a hashtable, so an indirect branch between a load-exclusive and store-exclusive, while perhaps unlikely to occur in most code, is another case where even without a tool adding additional instrumentation the core DynamoRIO engine has a potential problem.

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-156-

    -- /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-161-

    DynamoRIO must pretend that it is not perturbing the application's address space. An application bug that writes to invalid memory and generates an exception should do the same thing under DynamoRIO, even if we have allocated memory at that location that would natively have been invalid. This requires protecting all DynamoRIO memory from inadvertent (or malicious) writes by the application. Furthermore, DynamoRIO hides itself from introspection by manipulating memory queries.

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-162-
    Application Address Transparency
    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-163-[Omitted long context line] build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html:164:[Omitted long matching line] /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-165-

    Application errors under DynamoRIO must occur as they would natively. We accomplish this by maintaining Heap Transparency, Data Transparency, Stack Transparency, and Address Space Transparency, We have seen many cases of applications that access invalid memory natively, handle the exception, and carry on. Without Error Transparency such applications would not work properly under DynamoRIO.

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-166-
    Timing Transparency
    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/transparency.html-167-

    We would like to make it impossible for the application to determine whether it is executing inside of DynamoRIO. However, this may not be attainable for some aspects of execution, such as the exact timing of certain operations. This brings efficiency into the transparency equation.

    Returns a pointer to the application's Process Environment Block (PEB). DR swaps to a private PEB when running client code, in order to isolate the client and its dependent libraries from the application, so conventional methods of reading the PEB will obtain the private PEB instead of the application PEB.

    Note
    Windows only.

    Registers a callback function for the first instrumentation stage: application-to-application ("app2app") transformations on each basic block. drmgr will call func as the first of five instrumentation stages for each dynamic application basic block. Examples of app2app transformations include replacing one function with another or replacing one instruction with another throughout an application.

    /testbed/build_debug-internal-64/api/docs/html/group__drmgr.html-1343-

    The app2app passes are allowed to modify and insert non-meta (i.e., application) instructions and are intended for application code transformations. These passes should avoid adding meta instructions other than label instructions.

    The memory access traces contain some optimizations that combine references for one basic block together. This may result in not considering some thread interleavings that could occur natively. There are no other disruptions to thread ordering, however, and the application runs with all of its threads concurrently just like it would natively (although slower).

    Once every process has exited, the simulator prints cache miss statistics for each cache to stderr. The simulator is designed to be extensible, allowing for different cache studies to be carried out: see Extending the Simulator.

    install/docs/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-165-

    For L2 caching devices, the L1 caching devices are considered its children. Two separate miss rates are computed, one (the "Local miss rate") considering just requests that reach L2 while the other (the "Total miss rate") includes the child hits. This generalizes to deeper hierarchies: lower level caches are children and reported child hits are cumulative across all lower levels.

    /testbed/install/docs/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-166-

    For memory requests that cross blocks, each block touched is considered separately, resulting in separate hit and miss statistics. This can be changed by implementing a custom statistics gatherer (see Extending the Simulator).

    Even without a target tool like drcachesim's memory tracer inserting loads and stores, the core DynamoRIO instrumentation engine has several cases where it inserts memory operations between application instructions.

    /testbed/install/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-152-[Omitted long context line] install/docs/html/page_ldstex.html:153:[Omitted long matching line] /testbed/install/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-154-[Omitted long context line] /testbed/install/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-155-

    Additionally, application indirect branches under DynamoRIO load entries from a hashtable, so an indirect branch between a load-exclusive and store-exclusive, while perhaps unlikely to occur in most code, is another case where even without a tool adding additional instrumentation the core DynamoRIO engine has a potential problem.

    /testbed/install/docs/html/page_ldstex.html-156-

    -- /testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-161-

    DynamoRIO must pretend that it is not perturbing the application's address space. An application bug that writes to invalid memory and generates an exception should do the same thing under DynamoRIO, even if we have allocated memory at that location that would natively have been invalid. This requires protecting all DynamoRIO memory from inadvertent (or malicious) writes by the application. Furthermore, DynamoRIO hides itself from introspection by manipulating memory queries.

    /testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-162-
    Application Address Transparency
    /testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-163-[Omitted long context line] install/docs/html/transparency.html:164:[Omitted long matching line] /testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-165-

    Application errors under DynamoRIO must occur as they would natively. We accomplish this by maintaining Heap Transparency, Data Transparency, Stack Transparency, and Address Space Transparency, We have seen many cases of applications that access invalid memory natively, handle the exception, and carry on. Without Error Transparency such applications would not work properly under DynamoRIO.

    /testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-166-
    Timing Transparency
    /testbed/install/docs/html/transparency.html-167-

    We would like to make it impossible for the application to determine whether it is executing inside of DynamoRIO. However, this may not be attainable for some aspects of execution, such as the exact timing of certain operations. This brings efficiency into the transparency equation.

There are no other disruptions to thread ordering, however, and the application runs with all of its threads concurrently just like it would natively (although slower).

    install/docs_embed/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-91-

    Once every process has exited, the simulator prints cache miss statistics for each cache to stderr. The simulator is designed to be extensible, allowing for different cache studies to be carried out: see Extending the Simulator.

    install/docs_embed/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-92-

    For L2 caching devices, the L1 caching devices are considered its children. Two separate miss rates are computed, one (the "Local miss rate") considering just requests that reach L2 while the other (the "Total miss rate") includes the child hits. This generalizes to deeper hierarchies: lower level caches are children and reported child hits are cumulative across all lower levels.

    /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/sec_drcachesim_sim.html-93-

    For memory requests that cross blocks, each block touched is considered separately, resulting in separate hit and miss statistics. This can be changed by implementing a custom statistics gatherer (see Extending the Simulator).

    -- /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/transparency.html-88-

    DynamoRIO must pretend that it is not perturbing the application's address space. An application bug that writes to invalid memory and generates an exception should do the same thing under DynamoRIO, even if we have allocated memory at that location that would natively have been invalid. This requires protecting all DynamoRIO memory from inadvertent (or malicious) writes by the application. Furthermore, DynamoRIO hides itself from introspection by manipulating memory queries.

    /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/transparency.html-89-
    Application Address Transparency
    /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/transparency.html-90-[Omitted long context line] install/docs_embed/html/transparency.html:91:[Omitted long matching line] /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/transparency.html-92-

    Application errors under DynamoRIO must occur as they would natively. We accomplish this by maintaining Heap Transparency, Data Transparency, Stack Transparency, and Address Space Transparency, We have seen many cases of applications that access invalid memory natively, handle the exception, and carry on. Without Error Transparency such applications would not work properly under DynamoRIO.

    /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/transparency.html-93-
    Timing Transparency
    /testbed/install/docs_embed/html/transparency.html-94-

    We would like to make it impossible for the application to determine whether it is executing inside of DynamoRIO. However, this may not be attainable for some aspects of execution, such as the exact timing of certain operations. This brings efficiency into the transparency equation.

content.text: Based on my exploration of the codebase, I've found detailed information about the signal handling mechanism and the specific issue described. Let me provide a comprehensive summary of my findings: ## Signal Handling Architecture in DynamoRIO ### 1. Signal Handling Files Found - **Primary Signal Handler**: `/testbed/core/unix/signal.c` (primary implementation) - **Private Headers**: `/testbed/core/unix/signal_private.h` (signal data structures) - **Platform-specific**: Various signal_linux_*.c files for different architectures - **Related Components**: `/testbed/core/unix/vmareas.c`, `/testbed/core/heap.c`, `/testbed/core/unix/memquery_linux.c` ### 2. Pending Signal Mechanism and Limits **Key Configuration (max_pending_signals = 8)** - Found in `/testbed/core/optionsx.h` line 775: `OPTION_DEFAULT(uint, max_pending_signals, 8, "maximum count of pending signals per thread")` - Validation in `/testbed/core/options.c` lines 2025-2029: Minimum value of 1 enforced - Warning message defined in `/testbed/build_debug-internal-64/event_strings.h` line 22 **Pending Signal Implementation in `/testbed/core/unix/signal.c`:** ```c // Signal heap initialization (lines 706-712) info->sigheap = special_heap_init_aligned( pend_unit_size, IF_X86_ELSE(AVX_ALIGNMENT, 0), false /* cannot have any locking */, false /* -x */, true /* persistent */, pend_unit_size * DYNAMO_OPTION(max_pending_signals)); // Limit checks (lines 5257-5275) if (info->num_pending >= DYNAMO_OPTION(max_pending_signals)) { // Warning: "Reached the soft maximum number of pending signals" } ``` ### 3. Alarm Signal Special Handling (Dropped at 3rd Pending) **Found in `/testbed/core/unix/signal.c` around lines 5240-5252:** ```c if (sig_is_alarm_signal(sig) && info->sig[sig] != NULL) { /* drop 2nd pending alarm signal to avoid filling heap */ if (info->sigpending[sig]->next != NULL) { // 3rd pending alarm signal => dropping 2nd LOG(THREAD, LOG_ASYNCH, 2, "3rd pending alarm %d => dropping 2nd\n", sig); STATS_INC(num_signals_dropped); SYSLOG_INTERNAL_WARNING_ONCE("dropping 3rd pending alarm signal"); } } ``` ### 4. record_pending_signal Function This function is the main entry point for signal queuing at `/testbed/core/unix/signal.c:4817`: ```c static void record_pending_signal(dcontext_t *dcontext, int sig, kernel_ucontext_t *ucxt, sigframe_rt_t *frame, bool forged, byte *access_address) ``` Key responsibilities: - Determines whether to queue or immediately handle signals - Checks signal blocking status and thread masking - Manages the pending signal queue data structure - Handles alarm signal special cases ### 5. Signal Data Structure From `/testbed/core/unix/signal_private.h`: ```c typedef struct _sigpending_t { sigframe_rt_t rt_frame; byte *access_address; bool use_sigcontext; bool unblocked_at_receipt; struct _sigpending_t *next; // ... additional fields for frame state } sigpending_t; typedef struct _thread_sig_info_t { // ... various signal-related fields sigpending_t *sigpending[SIGARRAY_SIZE]; int num_pending; bool multiple_pending_units; bool accessing_sigpending; // ... signal heap and stack management } thread_sig_info_t; ``` ### 6. Lock Ordering Issue **Critical Lock Order Constraint:** The lock ordering issue revolves around the signal heap allocation when pending signals exceed the special heap unit capacity: **From `/testbed/core/unix/signal.c` lines 5261-5266:** ```c if (info->num_pending >= DYNAMO_OPTION(max_pending_signals)) { /* We're at the limit of our special heap: one more and it will try to * allocate a new unit, which is unsafe as it acquires locks. We take * several steps: we notify the user; we check for this on delivery as * well and proactively allocate a new unit in a safer context. * XXX: Perhaps we should drop some signals here? */ } ``` **Proactive Allocation (lines 7283-7294):** ```c if (!info->multiple_pending_units && info->num_pending + 2 >= DYNAMO_OPTION(max_pending_signals)) { /* We're close to the limit: proactively get a new unit while it's safe * to acquire locks. We do that by pushing over the edge. */ info->multiple_pending_units = true; sigpending_t *temp1 = special_heap_alloc(info->sigheap); // ... allocation and immediate freeing to trigger unit expansion } ``` ### 7. Lock Hierarchy Conflicts The lock ordering issue stems from: 1. **Signal handler context**: Cannot safely acquire mutex locks 2. **Heap allocation**: Normally requires locks for VM management 3. **Deadlock avoidance**: Enabled via `DEADLOCK_AVOIDANCE` (configured in debug builds) **Relevant locks involved:** - `memory_info_buf_lock` (in memquery_linux.c) - Heap management locks - Signal-specific locks ### 8. Related Files Analysis **memquery_linux.c**: Handles memory querying operations that might be called from signal handlers. Contains lock ordering comments about avoiding locks in signal contexts. **vmareas.c**: Manages virtual memory areas, contains lock-based operations that conflict with signal handler safety constraints. **heap.c**: General heap management with normal locking mechanisms unsuitable for signal contexts. ## Summary of the Issue The lock ordering issue occurs when: 1. Multiple signals arrive rapidly, filling the pending signal queue 2. The special signal heap (designed for lock-free operation) reaches capacity 3. Attempting to allocate beyond the pre-allocated unit triggers normal heap operations 4. Normal heap operations require locks that cannot be safely acquired in signal handler context 5. This creates a potential deadlock or unsafe lock acquisition scenario The code attempts to mitigate this by: - Setting a conservative default limit (8 pending signals) - Warning users when approaching the limit - Proactively expanding the heap in safer contexts - Special handling for alarm signals (dropping excess alarms) - Using lock-free special heaps for signal management However, the fundamental architectural constraint remains: signal handlers cannot safely perform operations that require normal mutex locks, creating a tension between signal queuing capacity and thread safety.
